text.title: Router architecture and the cost-quality knob
text.summary: How a preference-trained router actually works, the four model families that dominate the literature, and the single knob you ship to production.

section.title: What the router learns
section.p: The router is a tiny model that maps a prompt to a routing score in [0,1]. Score above the operating threshold means "use the strong model"; below means "use the weak model". It is trained on triples (prompt, weak_output, strong_output) labelled by which output a human or a judge LLM preferred.

rule.statement: Train the router on at least 10k preference triples sampled from your real traffic (or augment with Chatbot Arena pairs covering the same task families); split 80/10/10 train/val/test and tune the operating threshold on val.
rule.rationale: RouteLLM reports the matrix-factorization router needs roughly 80k Arena pairs to hit 95% GPT-4 quality at 26% strong-model traffic. Below ~10k the router learns noise and falls behind random.

example.code: from routellm.controller import Controller client = Controller( routers=["mf"], # matrix factorization strong_model="claude-opus-4", weak_model="claude-haiku-4-5", ) resp = client.chat.completions.create( model="router-mf-0.11593", # 0.11593 is the cost-quality knob messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}], )
example.note: Single call, one route, no cascade. The threshold (0.11593) was selected on the val set to hit 95% strong-model quality.
Brittle and unrouted: keyword rules do not generalize, miss easy hard-looking prompts and route trivial questions to Opus.

section.reference: RouteLLM: Learning to Route LLMs with Preference Data — Ong et al. 2024 (ICLR 2025).

list.item: Matrix factorization on (prompt-id, model-id, preference) — cheapest to train, strongest in the paper.
BERT classifier on prompt embedding → strong/weak — robust, ~2 ms per call.
Similarity ranker (kNN over labelled pairs) — works with very small datasets, no training step.
LLM-judge router (Haiku-as-judge picks weak vs strong from prompt only) — cheap baseline; lower quality than learned routers.

rule.statement: Pick the cheapest router family that hits your quality target on the val set; do not assume the largest router wins.
rule.rationale: RouteLLM ablations show matrix factorization beats BERT and LLM-judge on the public benchmark while being orders of magnitude cheaper to serve.

section.title: The cost-quality knob
section.p: Every preference router exposes one scalar threshold. Sweep it on the val set to produce a Pareto curve (strong-model fraction vs preserved quality) and ship the operating point your business wants.

rule.statement: Ship the threshold that preserves at least 95% of strong-only quality on the val set, then verify on the held-out test set before rollout.
rule.rationale: Tuning on test inflates measured quality; per ICLR-2025 reproductions, 5-10 pp regressions are routine when teams skip the held-out check.

antipattern.description: One universal threshold across very different traffic slices (B2B chat vs internal code agents).
antipattern.reason: Optimal threshold differs per slice; one global threshold under-routes one and over-routes another.

section.reference: Minimal Controller wrapper that wires the threshold into a per-call route.

see-also.link: How to keep the router honest after rollout.
